subject: Physics

topics: Activities: To measure the resistance and impedance of an inductor with or without iron core.
Activities: To measure resistance, voltage (AC/DC), current (AC) and check continuity of a given circuit using multimeter.
Activities: To assemble a household circuit comprising three bulbs, three (on/off) switches, a fuse and a power source.
Activities: To assemble the components of a given electrical circuit.
Activities: To study the variation in potential drop with length of a wire for a steady current.

Activities: To draw the diagram of a given open circuit comprising at least a battery, resistor/rheostat, key, ammeter and voltmeter. Mark the components that are not connected in proper order and correct the circuit and also the circuit diagram.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the value of v for different values of u in case of a concave mirror and to find the focal length.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a convex mirror, using a convex lens.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a convex lens by plotting graphs between u and v or between 1/u and 1/v .
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a concave lens, using a convex lens.
Experiments SECTION–B: To determine angle of minimum deviation for a given prism by plotting a graph between angle of incidence and angle of deviation.
Experiments SECTION–B: To determine refractive index of a glass slab using a travelling microscope.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the refractive index of a liquid using convex lens and plane mirror.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the refractive index of a liquid using a concave mirror and a plane mirror.
Experiments SECTION–B: To draw the I-V characteristic curve for a p-n junction diode in forward and reverse bias.
Activities SECTION–B: To identify a diode, an LED, a resistor and a capacitor from a mixed collection of such items.
Activities SECTION–B: Use of multimeter to see the unidirectional flow of current in case of a diode and an LED and check whether a given electronic component (e.g., diode) is in working order.
Activities SECTION–B: To study effect of intensity of light (by varying distance of the source) on an LDR.
Activities SECTION–B: To observe refraction and lateral deviation of a beam of light incident obliquely on a glass slab.
Activities SECTION–B: To observe diffraction of light due to a thin slit.

Activities SECTION–B: To study the nature and size of the image formed by a (i) convex lens, or (ii) concave mirror, on a screen by using a candle and a screen (for different distances of the candle from the lens/mirror).
Activities SECTION–B: To obtain a lens combination with the specified focal length by using two lenses from the given set of lenses.
subtopics: Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.

topics: Record of at least 8 Experiments [with 4 from each section], to be performed by the students.
Record of at least 6 Activities [with 3 each from section A and section B], to be performed by the students.
The Report of the project carried out by the students.
Evaluation Scheme: Max. Marks: 30 Time 3 hours
Two experiments one from each section 7+7 Marks
Practical record [experiments and activities] 5 Marks
One activity from any section 3 Marks
Investigatory Project 3 Marks
Viva on experiments, activities and project 5 Marks
Total 30 marks
subtopics: Experiments SECTION–A: To determine resistivity of two / three wires by plotting a graph for potential difference versus current.
Experiments SECTION–A: To find resistance of a given wire / standard resistor using metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To verify the laws of combination (series) of resistances using a metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To verify the laws of combination (parallel) of resistances using a metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To determine resistance of a galvanometer by half-deflection method and to find its figure of merit.
Experiments SECTION–A: To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into a voltmeter of desired range and to verify the same.
Experiments SECTION–A: To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into an ammeter of desired range and to verify the same.
Experiments SECTION–A: To find the frequency of AC mains with a sonometer.

previousSessionContext: Chapter–1: Electric Charges and Fields: We learned that charge is quantized, conserved, and creates an electric field.

sourceSessionPlan.title: Chapter–1: Electric Charges and Fields

teacherLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session introduces the fundamental concept of electric charge, its properties (quantization, conservation), and the nature of electrostatic forces. Students will explore how charges interact through fields, moving from macroscopic observations to microscopic explanations.
prerequisiteKnowledge: Basic understanding of matter composition (atoms, protons, electrons)
previousSessionRecap: Atoms contain positive nuclei and negative electrons
learningOutcomes: Define electric charge and state its two types (positive/negative)
Explain the principle of conservation of charge
Describe the quantization of charge (q = ne)
Distinguish between conductors, insulators, and semiconductors

teachingPlan.teachingStrategy: Demonstration with a balloon and hair to spark curiosity about invisible forces.
Direct instruction with real-life analogies (coins for quantization).
Visual explanation of charge distribution in conductors vs. insulators.
Quick oral quiz and linking to Coulomb's Law.

lessonPurpose: To establish the foundation for all electrostatic phenomena
To explain why objects attract or repel without touching
To introduce the mathematical language of charge (q=ne)
teachingSequence: Hook: Rub a balloon on hair and ask 'Why does it stick?'
Define Charge: Introduce + and - types using the gold leaf electroscope concept.
Core Property 1: Like charges repel, unlike attract.
Core Property 2: Conservation (Total charge before = Total charge after).
Core Property 3: Quantization (Charge comes in packets of electron charge).
Material Classification: Conductors (free flow), Insulators (fixed), Semiconductors (controlled).
Field Concept: Introduce the idea of a 'region of influence' around a charge.
guidedPractice: Calculate total charge if 5 electrons are removed from a neutral body.
Identify if a plastic rod rubbed with wool is positive or negative and why.
Predict the behavior of two charged spheres brought close together.

lessonBlocks.title: Introduction to Electric Charge
teacherPrompt: Have you ever felt a shock when touching a doorknob after walking on a carpet? What caused that?
explanation: We will define electric charge as an intrinsic property of matter that causes it to experience a force in an electromagnetic field.
There are two types: Positive (protons) and Negative (electrons).
examples: Rubbing a glass rod with silk (glass becomes positive).
Rubbing a plastic rod with wool (plastic becomes negative).
boardWork: Definition: Charge is the property of matter that causes electrostatic force.
boardSteps: Draw two circles: one labeled '+' and one labeled '-' with arrows showing repulsion.
solutionFlow: State that charge is a scalar quantity.
proofSteps: Observation: Rubbed objects attract/repel without contact.
Conclusion: An invisible property called 'charge' exists.
checkUnderstanding: If I rub a balloon on my hair, what happens to the electrons?
expectedAnswers: Electrons move from hair to the balloon.
activity: Quick pair-share: Describe one time you experienced static electricity.

lessonBlocks.title: Quantization and Conservation
teacherPrompt: Can we have half an electron's worth of charge? Why or why not?
explanation: Charge is quantized: q = n * e, where 'e' is the elementary charge (1.6 x 10^-19 C) and 'n' is an integer.
Conservation of Charge: In an isolated system, total charge remains constant.
examples: Counting coins: You can have 1 coin or 2 coins, but not 1.5 coins (analogy for quantization).
Transferring electrons from one body to another increases one's negative charge and decreases the other's.
boardWork: Law: Total Charge Before = Total Charge After
boardSteps: Draw a diagram of two neutral spheres, then show electrons moving from one to the other.
proofSteps: Fact: Electrons are indivisible in electrostatic contexts.
Application: Charge transfer preserves the net sum.
expectedAnswers: No, because n must be an integer.
activity: Mini-calculation: Calculate the charge on a body with 10^19 excess electrons.

conceptFlow.definition: Charge exists in discrete packets equal to integer multiples of the elementary charge.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Just as you cannot have half a coin, you cannot have half an electron's charge. All observable charges are q = ne.
conceptFlow.importance: It explains why charge is not continuous and sets the scale for atomic interactions.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: To calculate exact charges in problems and understand atomic structure.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Builds on the knowledge of protons and electrons.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Leads to Coulomb's Law and Electric Fields.
teacherMoves: Emphasize that 'n' cannot be a fraction.
examples: A body with +2C has 1.25 x 10^19 protons more than electrons.
visuals: Stack of coins vs. continuous water flow
solutionFlow: Divide by e to find n.
Check if n is an integer.

conceptFlow.definition: The total electric charge in an isolated system remains constant over time.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Charge cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred from one object to another.
conceptFlow.importance: It is a fundamental law of physics applicable to all electrostatic phenomena.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: To solve problems involving charge transfer and neutralization.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Relies on the existence of positive and negative charges.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Essential for analyzing circuits and electromagnetic waves.
teacherMoves: Show a neutral object becoming positive and another negative simultaneously.
examples: Rubbing glass with silk: Glass loses electrons (+), Silk gains (-). Net change = 0.
visuals: Balance scale showing equal and opposite charges
proofSteps: Observation: No net charge is generated in friction.

classroomQuestions.question: Why do we say charge is quantized?
classroomQuestions.expectedResponse: Because it comes in multiples of the electron charge.

classroomQuestions.question: If a body has a charge of -3.2 x 10^-19 C, how many excess electrons does it have?

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Charge can be created out of nothing during rubbing.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Rubbing only transfers existing electrons; the total charge remains zero.

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Quantization is noticeable in everyday macroscopic objects.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: The electron charge is so small that for large charges, it appears continuous (like sand looking like a fluid).

slowLearners: Provide a formula sheet with e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C.
averageLearners: Practice simple integer calculations for q=ne.
advancedLearners: Discuss why quantization is not visible in macroscopic circuits (large n).

teacherTips: Use a balloon demonstration to make the abstract concrete.
Write 'q=ne' on the board and keep it visible throughout the class.
misconceptions: Charge is a fluid that flows
formativeChecks: Ask students to hold up fingers: 1 for positive, 2 for negative when I name an object.
assessmentQuestions: State the principle of conservation of charge with an example.
sessionSummary: Electric charge is quantized (q=ne) and conserved in isolated systems.

endOfClassRecap.prompt: Charge is ________ by integer multiples of the electron charge.

nextSessionBridge: Now that we know what charge is, how do these charges interact? Next: Coulomb's Law.

learningOutcomes: Define electric charge and its types.
Explain the quantization of charge using q=ne.
Apply the law of conservation of charge to simple scenarios.
Differentiate between conductors, insulators, and semiconductors.
sourceSessionPlan.introduction: Imagine holding a balloon. You rub it on your hair, and suddenly it sticks to the wall without any glue! What invisible force is holding it there? Today, we unlock the mystery of 'Electric Charge'—the fundamental property that makes this magic happen. We will discover why charge comes in tiny packets and how it never disappears, only moves.

theory.overview: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. There are two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Like charges repel, and unlike charges attract.
keyPoints: Charge is quantized: q = ne, where n is an integer.
Total charge in an isolated system is conserved.
Materials are classified as conductors, insulators, or semiconductors based on charge mobility.
theory.detailedContent: The concept of electric charge explains phenomena ranging from lightning to the stability of atoms. When we rub a glass rod with silk, electrons move from the glass to the silk. The glass becomes positively charged (lost electrons), and the silk becomes negatively charged (gained electrons). The total charge remains zero. This is the Law of Conservation of Charge. Furthermore, charge is not continuous; it comes in discrete packets called 'quanta'. The smallest unit is the charge of an electron (e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C). Any observable charge q must be an integer multiple of e (q = ne). This quantization is usually not visible in daily life because the number of electrons involved is huge, making the charge appear continuous. However, at the atomic level, it is strictly discrete.

activities.name: The Balloon Experiment
instructions: Students rub a balloon on their hair or wool sweater.
Observe how the balloon sticks to the wall or hair.
Discuss: Did we create charge, or move it? Which way did electrons go?

activities.name: Charge Calculator Challenge
instructions: Teacher provides a list of charges (e.g., -3.2 x 10^-19 C, +4.8 x 10^-19 C).
Students calculate the number of excess/deficit electrons for each.
Verify if the result is an integer.

studentLessonNotes.title: Electric Charges and Fields: The Invisible Force
studentLessonNotes.sessionOverview: Explore the fundamental nature of electric charge, how charges interact through electric fields, and the laws governing these invisible forces.
studentLessonNotes.introduction: Welcome to Chapter 1! Today, we uncover the secrets behind why your hair stands up after taking off a woolen cap or how lightning strikes. We will dive into the world of 'Electric Charges' and the invisible 'Electric Fields' that surround them. You don't need to be a superhero to understand this; you just need to know how nature's basic building blocks interact.
learningObjectives: Understand what electric charge is and its fundamental properties (quantization and conservation).
Explain Coulomb's Law and calculate the force between two point charges.
Define an Electric Field and visualize how it acts on other charges.
Distinguish between positive and negative charges and their interactions.
quickRecall: Matter is made of atoms containing protons (+) and electrons (-).
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Force causes a change in motion (Newton's Laws).
easyToRemember: Charge is quantized: It comes in tiny, indivisible packets of 'e'.
Field lines flow from Positive to Negative.

comparisonTables.title: Properties of Electric Charge vs. Mass
rows: Total mass is conserved (in classical physics)

formulaCards.title: Coulomb's Law
formulaCards.meaning: The force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
formulaCards.whenToUse: When calculating the electrostatic force between two stationary point charges in a vacuum or air.

formulaCards.title: Electric Field Intensity
formulaCards.meaning: The electric field at a point is the force experienced per unit positive test charge placed at that point.
formulaCards.whenToUse: When determining the strength and direction of an electric field created by a source charge.

geometryDiagrams.title: Electric Field Lines around a Positive Charge
geometryDiagrams.caption: Visualizing the invisible electric field radiating outward from a positive point charge.

proofSteps: Start with the observation that force increases as charges get closer.
Observe that doubling the distance reduces the force to one-fourth (Inverse Square Law).
Conclude that Force is proportional to q1 * q2 and inversely proportional to r^2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking electric field lines can cross each other.
commonMistakes.correction: Field lines never intersect because a charge cannot experience force in two different directions at the same point.
commonMistakes.example: If they crossed, a test charge would move in two directions simultaneously, which is impossible.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting that Coulomb's law applies only to point charges.
commonMistakes.correction: For extended objects (like rods or spheres), you must integrate or use specific formulas for their geometry.
commonMistakes.example: Do not use F = kq1q2/r^2 directly for two large charged metal plates.

sections.heading: What is Electric Charge?
sections.explanation: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. Think of it as the 'electric personality' of a particle.
keyPoints: There are two types: Positive (+) and Negative (-).
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Charge is quantized (comes in discrete packets).
examples: Rubbing a glass rod with silk gives it a positive charge.
Rubbing a plastic rod with wool gives it a negative charge.
sections.whyItMatters: Without charge, there would be no electricity, no atoms holding together, and no life as we know it.
sections.detailedExplanation: Charge is not continuous; it comes in multiples of the elementary charge 'e' (charge of an electron). Any charge Q must be Q = n*e, where n is an integer. Also, charge can never be created or destroyed, only transferred.
observedIn: Hair standing up after removing a hat
visualSupport: Diagram showing electrons moving from wool to plastic rod.

visualAssets.prompt: A simple illustration of a plastic rod and a piece of wool. Arrows show small negative dots (electrons) moving from the wool to the rod, leaving the wool positive and the rod negative.
visualAssets.alt: Electron transfer diagram showing static electricity generation

importantNotes: Charge is always conserved in an isolated system.
The unit of charge is the Coulomb (C).
memoryTechniques: Remember: 'P' for Positive and 'N' for Negative. Opposites attract like a magnet.
conceptSummary: Charge is quantized, conserved, and creates forces between objects.

sections.heading: Coulomb's Law: The Force of Attraction
sections.explanation: This law tells us exactly how strong the push or pull is between two charged particles. It depends on how big the charges are and how far apart they are.
keyPoints: Force is directly proportional to the product of charges (q1 * q2).
Force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance (1/r^2).
The force acts along the line joining the two charges.
examples: If you double the distance between two charges, the force becomes 1/4th as strong.
If you double one charge, the force doubles.
sections.whyItMatters: It is the foundation of electrostatics, just like Newton's Law of Gravitation is for gravity.
sections.detailedExplanation: The formula F = k(q1q2)/r^2 shows that if you move charges further apart, the force drops very quickly. This is why static shocks happen when things are close but vanish when they are far away.
observedIn: Combing hair near a TV screen
visualSupport: Graph showing Force vs. Distance (inverse square curve).

visualAssets.prompt: A graph with 'Distance' on the x-axis and 'Force' on the y-axis. A curve starts high on the left and drops rapidly to the right, labeled 'Inverse Square Law'.
visualAssets.alt: Graph illustrating Coulomb's inverse square law

importantNotes: This law applies strictly to stationary point charges.
memoryTechniques: Remember: 'Far away, weak force. Close up, strong force.'
conceptSummary: Coulomb's Law defines the magnitude of electrostatic force between two point charges.

sections.heading: The Electric Field: An Invisible Web
sections.explanation: A charge doesn't just 'touch' another charge; it creates a field around itself. This field is like an invisible web that tells other charges how to move.
keyPoints: Electric Field (E) is Force per unit charge (F/q).
Direction: Away from positive, towards negative.
examples: Imagine a magnet; the field lines are like the invisible lines of force around it.
A test charge placed in the field feels a push or pull.
sections.whyItMatters: Fields explain how forces act at a distance without physical contact.
sections.detailedExplanation: If you place a positive charge in space, it creates an electric field. If you bring another positive charge near, the field pushes it away. The density of field lines shows the strength of the field.
visualSupport: Diagram of field lines radiating from a positive charge and converging on a negative charge.

visualAssets.prompt: Two charges: a large '+' on the left and a large '-' on the right. Curved arrows (field lines) flow from '+' to '-', showing the direction of the electric field.

importantNotes: The electric field is a vector quantity (has magnitude and direction).
Inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium, the electric field is zero.
conceptSummary: The electric field is the region around a charge where other charges experience a force.

definitions.definition: The principle that total charge on any body is an integral multiple of the elementary charge (e). Q = ne.
The force experienced by a unit positive test charge placed at a point in an electric field.
An idealized charge with negligible size compared to the distance from it, treated as a single point.

workedExamples.title: Calculating Force Between Two Charges
workedExamples.problem: Two point charges q1 = +2 μC and q2 = -3 μC are placed 0.5 m apart in air. Calculate the magnitude of the force between them.
steps: Identify the values and convert units to Coulombs.
Substitute values into Coulomb's Law formula.
reasoning: We use the magnitude of charges because we are calculating force strength.
The negative sign indicates attraction, but the magnitude is positive.
workedExamples.explanation: First, we convert microcoulombs to Coulombs. Then we plug everything into the formula. Since the distance is squared in the denominator, a small distance leads to a larger force.

conceptMap: Charge -> Quantized -> Creates Field -> Exerts Force
quickRecap: Force drops with square of distance.

selfCheckQuestions: If the distance between two charges is doubled, what happens to the force?
Can a body have a charge of 1.5e? Why or why not?
In which direction do electric field lines point relative to a negative charge?
quickSummary: Electric charges interact via forces described by Coulomb's Law.
These forces are mediated by an Electric Field.

fillInTheBlanks.prompt: The total charge of an isolated system is always ______.
Electric field lines originate from a ______ charge and terminate at a ______ charge.

mcqQuestions.question: Which of the following is true about electric charge?
options: C. It depends on mass only

mcqQuestions.question: If the distance between two charges is halved, the force becomes:

veryShortAnswerQuestions.question: Define quantization of charge.
veryShortAnswerQuestions.answer: Charge exists in discrete packets equal to integer multiples of the elementary charge (e).

veryShortAnswerQuestions.question: What is the SI unit of electric charge?

didYouKnow: The total positive charge in one proton is exactly equal to the magnitude of the negative charge in one electron.
summary: We learned that charge is quantized, conserved, and creates electric fields.

homework.title: Quantization of Charge Calculation
homework.instructions: Calculate the total charge on an object that has an excess of 50 electrons. Use the elementary charge value e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.
homework.question: An insulating sphere carries a net negative charge due to an excess of 50 electrons. Calculate the magnitude and sign of this total charge.

homework.title: Conservation of Charge Explanation
homework.instructions: Explain the principle of conservation of charge in your own words. Provide one real-life example where this principle is observed.
homework.question: State the law of conservation of electric charge. Illustrate it with an example involving the transfer of electrons between two neutral objects.
homework.expectedResponse: Law: Total charge in an isolated system remains constant. Example: When a glass rod is rubbed with silk, the positive charge on the rod equals the negative charge on the silk; net charge remains zero.

homework.title: Electric Field Lines Representation
homework.instructions: Draw the electric field lines for a single positive point charge and a single negative point charge. Label the direction of the field lines.
homework.question: Sketch the pattern of electric field lines around (a) an isolated positive point charge (+q) and (b) an isolated negative point charge (-q). Indicate the direction of the field vectors on your diagram.
homework.answerSpace: Draw two separate diagrams. Label 'Positive Charge' and 'Negative Charge'.
homework.visualRequirement: Draw radial lines originating from positive charge (outward) and terminating at negative charge (inward). Lines should be straight and symmetric.
homework.expectedResponse: Diagram showing outward radial lines for +q and inward radial lines for -q.

homework.title: Real-World Connection: Static Electricity
homework.instructions: Read the scenario below and answer the questions based on the concepts of charge quantization and electric fields.
homework.question: Scenario: A student rubs a plastic comb through dry hair. The comb attracts small pieces of paper. (a) Explain why the comb becomes charged. (b) If the comb gains 10¹⁰ electrons, calculate the total charge acquired. (c) Describe the direction of the electric field lines around the comb.
homework.visualRequirement: Describe the field lines in your answer for part (c).
homework.expectedResponse: (a) Transfer of electrons from hair to comb. (b) Q = 10¹⁰ × (-1.6×10⁻¹⁹) = -1.6×10⁻⁹ C. (c) Field lines point inward towards the negatively charged comb.

homework.title: Misconception Check: Charge and Mass
homework.instructions: Address the following statement: 'When an object is charged, its mass changes significantly.' Is this true or false? Justify your answer using the concept of quantization.
homework.question: Critique the statement: 'Charging a body by rubbing it with another body results in a negligible change in mass.' Do you agree? Explain why the mass change is usually ignored in physics problems despite electrons having mass.
homework.expectedResponse: Agree. Electrons have very small mass (9.1×10⁻³¹ kg). Even for macroscopic charges, the total mass change is negligible compared to the object's total mass.
